text: The First Commandment in the Heidelberg Catechism This article looks at how the first commandment is explained in the Heidelberg Catechism (HC). For each Dutch question and statement, the reconstructed German and Latin versions are provided. Since Zacharias Ursinus, the main author of the HC, was a student of Philipp Melanchthon in Wittenberg, two of Melanchthon’s influential treatises are examined to lay the groundwork for understanding the theological and exgetical matter underlying the Catechism. In addition, the article will also take a closer look at Zacharias Ursinus’ understanding of the first commandment in his Small and Large Catechisms as well as in his dogmatics lectures. In combination, these elements should provide insight into the reasons why the first commandment was placed where it is in the Heidelberg Catechism.
explanation: The passage contains domain-specific academic content with precise references (Heidelberg Catechism, Ursinus, Melanchthon) in a natural scholarly tone. This specificity is more typical of human authorship, though AI could emulate it.
